preamble: The shift from the postalveolar fricative [ʃ] to the alveolar fricative [s] is known as alveolarization or depalatalization. It is a common feature in language evolution, often occurring through systematic mergers (as in Semitic languages) or as a way to simplify phonetic inventories.

examples.note: Standard correspondence between Hebrew [ʃ] and Arabic [s] from Proto-Semitic.
In many languages that lack a postalveolar fricative, borrowed [ʃ] is adapted as [s].
